summary: Jazz‑heavy second set with soaring horns and killer Keith solo
blurb: Second set delivers a jazz‑laden journey featuring an explosive Eyes of the World, Let It Grow jam and Keith Godchaux’s standout solo on Let Me Sing Your Blues Away. Jerry’s vocals are praised as “blow me away” and his guitar shines on Bird Song and Big River. The first set is solid but audio‑limited.
review: On September 15, 1973 at the Providence Civic Center the Dead slipped into a deep jazz‑fusion groove that still feels fresh. While the first set rolls along with classic material, it’s the second set that steals the show: Truckin' segues into an expansive Eyes Of The World, then into Keith Godchaux’s killer solo on Let Me Sing Your Blues Away, followed by a soaring Weather Report Suite and a marathon Let It Grow that pushes the band to high‑energy heights before easing into Stella Blue and a rousing Sugar Magnolia. Jerry’s voice is described as “blow me away,” his lead guitar “flaming” on Bird Song and Big River, and the horn section carries the set with bright, adventurous lines. The audience gets a taste of the era’s classic soundboard clarity in the matrix‑SBD hybrid recording, making this night a must‑listen for fans of the band’s 1973 jazz period.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Both recordings are marked positive; reviewers consistently praise the second‑set jazz heavy vibe, Keith’s solo, soaring horns, and Jerry’s vocal/guitar performance while noting no significant negative comments about playing. This strong, repeated enthusiasm across multiple sources justifies a high positive sentiment adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard capture of the entire second set with classic ’73 board clarity and no drop‑outs, delivering the full jazz‑fusion experience.

key_highlights: Jazz‑heavy second set with soaring horn lines throughout.
Keith Godchaux’s killer solo on “Let Me Sing Your Blues Away”.
Monster Eyes Of The World and expansive Let It Grow jam.
Jerry’s vocals described as “blow me away” and guitar “flaming” on Bird Song.
song_highlights: Let Me Sing Your Blues Away

band_performance.Jerry: Vocals are praised as “blow me away” and his lead guitar is called “flaming” on Bird Song, Big River and throughout the Let It Grow jam.
band_performance.Phil: Bass provides a solid foundation; a few listeners note it sounds slightly light in the mix but no performance issues are mentioned.
band_performance.Keith: Delivers a killer solo on “Let Me Sing Your Blues Away” and helps drive the jazz‑fusion vibe of the second set, even taking lead vocals on that tune according to some reviewers.

must_listen_sequences: Let Me Sing Your Blues Away
